Tafseer:
The oath by the heaven and the stars:
Just as an oath was sworn by the heaven containing towers in the previous Surah, an oath is sworn in this Surah by the heaven and its bright stars. This oath, as clarified, serves as a testimony to prove the claim coming next.
'Tariq' literally means a night-comer, but here it refers to the stars that appear at night. The Qur'an itself has clarified this by the words: 'The piercing star' (an-najmu-th-thaqib) (verse 3).

The question 'And what can make you know' (wa ma adrak) is intended to draw attention to the grandeur and importance of this testimony. The meaning is: no one should regard this testimony as minor. It is a highly significant testimony, provided those who reflect do so, instead of trying to mock it.

'The piercing star' (an-najmu-th-thaqib) does not refer to a specific star, but is used generically, just as: (and by the stars they guide themselves) (An-Nahl, 16:16) and other similar verses. However, the attribute 'thaqib' (piercing) indicates that it refers to those stars whose light reaches us on its own, without needing advanced telescopes. Every observer can see them and learn the lesson the Qur'an intends to convey here. It should noted that the stars are not limited to those we see. These are only a small sample. Who besides Allah Almighty can know how many worlds and stars exist!

The testimony of the stars to the claim that there is a guardian over every soul:
This is the subject of the oath (muqsam 'alayh), or the core claim, to prove which the aforementioned oath was sworn. 
Using 'lamma' [meaning 'except'] here is somewhat rare; therefore, the grammarians have differed regarding its analysis. We explained this under Surah Hud, verse 111, and Surah Az-Zukhruf, verse 35.
The testimony of the stars to the claim that Allah Almighty has appointed guardians over every soul is first from the perspective that man should reflect: 'The God Who has appointed such countless eyes that remain awake all night, staring at the inhabitants of the earth, who can dare to escape His grasp.' Even the most powerful telescopes invented by science do not possess the power of the most ordinary stars, whose light pierces through layers of atmosphere to reach the earth. The person who develops the concept that anything can remain hidden from His sight—while His power displays this wonder to us every night—is completely devoid of intellect. 
The second perspective is what we pointed out in the previous Surah, and which has appeared in other places—namely: Allah has established towers within these stars from which shooting stars are pelted at the devils who dare to cross His forbidden boundaries. This arrangement of nature bears witness that this world is not a flock without a shepherd; rather, God has appointed His guardians at every corner, supervising day and night. Therefore, a Day of Account must inevitably come, from whose reckoning no one can save himself.

Man's existence is a witness that God can recreate him:
This points to the sense of responsibility that should develop in every person who realizes that this world is not an unjust place, but its Creator is observing everything. Since He is observing, He must inevitably bring a Day when the virtues of the righteous and the evils of the wicked appear in their true results.
'So let man observe from what he was created' (fa-l-yandhuri-l-insanu mimma khuliq) - This draws attention to his own existence: if he reflects on his own creation, the power, wisdom, and craftsmanship of God will appear, and he will confess that the God Who can create him from an insignificant drop of water with these high capabilities can easily recreate him after death.
The primary doubt of the deniers of the Resurrection was that they regarded being recreated after death as impossible. To clear this doubt, the Qur'an repeatedly invited them to reflect on their own creation. In Surah 'Abasa, He warned such people:
(May man be destroyed! How ungrateful he is! From what substance did He create him? From a sperm-drop He created him and proportioned him; then the way He eased for him; then He causes him to die and provides a grave for him. Then when He wills, He will resurrect him.) ('Abasa, 80:17-22).

'He was created from a fluid, ejected, emerging from between the backbone and the ribs' (khuliqa mim-ma'in-dafiq, yakhruju mim-bayni-s-sulbi wa-t-tara'ib) - This clarifies the nature of this water, the manner of its exit, and the area of its origin, so that man realizes: this water is neither a rare gem nor does it come from some territory outside God's sovereignty. It is ejected from between his backbone and ribs, and His power shapes it in His mold according to His will, and then brings it forth from the womb. The meaning is: will the God Whose power and wisdom every person witnesses within his own existence find it difficult to recreate him! A similar style appeared in Surah Al-Infitar:
(O man, what has deceived you concerning your Lord, the Generous, Who created you, then proportioned you, and balanced you? In whatever form He willed, He assembled you. No! But you deny the Recompense) (Al-Infitar, 82:6-9).
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 6 and 7 was combined in the original text.]

'Indeed, He, to return him, is Able' (innahu 'ala raj'ihi laqadir) - That is: the God Who displayed His power and wisdom in human creation is certainly powerful over recreating him after he has turned to dust. In 'to return him' (raj'ihi), the reference of the pronoun is 'man' (al-insan) who was invited to reflect in verse 5: 'So let man observe from what he was created'. It should noted that although the wording is general, the focus is specifically on the Quraysh, who fabricated such doubts to devalue the Qur'an's warning.

The nature of the Hereafter's reckoning:
This describes the reckoning people will face on that Day. It is stated: on that Day, secrets will be tested. That is: not only open words and deeds will be discussed, but hidden deeds, inner states, and motives will also be tested. Allah Almighty has the means to discover what was performed in the most hidden corners, testing every deed to see how much hypocrisy or sincerity it contains. Note that on that Day, a person's limbs will testify to what he did. When his own limbs rise as witnesses against him, what possibility remains for any secret to be hidden?

On that Day, man will have no personal strength to defend himself, nor will any helper or intercessor rise to support him. Everyone's outer and inner states will be open like a book, and only the decision of the All-Knowing and All-Aware God will be executed.

Historical testimony to life after death:
Above, life after death and the Resurrection were proved through human creation. Now, attention is drawn to an argument from nature in support of the same claim.
'Raj'' (return/rain) is explained by the lexicographers as 'the rain after rain'—referring to the rain that falls continuously and revives the earth.

'And [by] the earth which splits open' (wa-l-ardi dhati-s-sad') - 'Sad'' means splitting. That is: when rain falls, the earth's pores open, and it absorbs water, swells, and revives with lush vegetation. Although the growing of vegetation is not explicitly mentioned, context indicates it. This is an oath, so the matter is stated in brief.
In other places, the Qur'an has used different styles to prove that resurrecting people after death is not difficult for Allah Almighty, rebuking those who doubted this daily witnessed occurrence. In Surah Al-Anbiya, He warned such doubters:
(Have those who disbelieved not considered that the heavens and the earth were a joined-up mass, and We split them asunder? And We made from water every living thing) (Al-Anbiya, 21:30).
We explained this verse: the joining of heaven and earth means rain does not fall and vegetation does not grow; but when Allah opens the windows of heaven to send rain, the earth also opens the doors of its treasures. Similarly, He can resurrect all those buried in the earth whenever He wills.

Warning to the deniers of the Resurrection:
Just as the previous Surah concluded by warning the deniers not to mock the Qur'an's warning, since it is a noble speech revealed from the Preserved Tablet, this Surah also concludes with a new style: the Resurrection of which the Qur'an is warning is a decisive and certain event that you must face; so do not mock it, but wisdom requires you to listen, understand, and prepare for it.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 13 and 14 was combined in the original text.]

Comfort for the Prophet:
This comforts the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): the doubts they raise to mock your warning are not because they actually have any questions that can be cleared by an answer or a miracle. These doubts are fabricated solely to flee from reality—meaning they neither want to accept your words themselves nor let their people accept them, so that their leadership remains and they can continue to follow their desires.

'But I am planning a plan' (wa akeedu kayda) - It is stated: to counter their plot, I am also planning a plan. That is: instead of hastening their seizure, I am granting them respite so that they may fill their cup of sins fully, leaving them with no excuse when they are seized. These foolish individuals regard this respite as success, whereas they are caught in His gradual leading (istidraj) in such a manner that the longer their rope is extended, the firmer His grasp becomes. This is identical to: (And Allah, from behind them, encompasses) (Al-Buruj, 85:20) in the previous Surah.

Since they are encompassed by Allah Almighty from all sides, even if they are granted respite, there is no fear of their escaping His grasp; so you also grant them respite. That is: do not desire that they be shown a sign of punishment immediately, or that they be destroyed right away.
'So allow them respite for a little' (amhilhum ruwayda) - This explains the preceding point: this respite does not mean they will be spared forever, but their rope is extended for a little so they can perform whatever they wish, for ultimately they must face their end.
Note the style: the Prophet is commanded to grant them respite, which reveals that their destiny has been placed in the Prophet's hand. However, He prefers that the Prophet grant them a little more respite.
